findings.reasoning: The page discusses a 20% discount offer on TurboTax federal filing, product tiers (Do It Yourself, Expert Full Service, Business Taxes), guarantees, and offer details. There is no mention of 'TurboTax Free', 'Free Edition', or a $0 free filing product anywhere in the visible page text. The footer navigation link list does include a link labeled 'Free Edition tax filing' and 'Free military tax filing discount', but these are simply navigational menu links to other pages (standard site footer navigation), not a marketing mention of the Free product on this page's actual content. The strongest counter-reading is that the footer nav link 'Free Edition tax filing' constitutes a 'mention' of TurboTax Free, which would trigger the rule requiring the eligibility disclosure right underneath it. However, this is a generic sitewide footer navigation link (present on nearly all TurboTax pages) that merely routes to the Free Edition product page, not a substantive marketing claim or offer promoting the free product within this page's content, and it is not accompanied by any characterization of free filing that would need substantiation via the disclosure. Requiring the ~37% disclosure under every footer nav link across the entire site would be an unreasonable extension of the rule's intent, which is to disclose eligibility limitations wherever the free product is marketed/promoted as an offer. Treating this as non-triggering is the more defensible reading, so the rule is not triggered on this page.

findings.location: footer navigation links under “Online Software Products”
findings.reasoning: The rule triggers whenever TurboTax Free is mentioned. The footer includes the link text “Free Edition tax filing,” which is TurboTax’s branded free product. That constitutes a mention of TurboTax Free for purposes of R-01. Immediately underneath that link there is no disclosure about eligibility or filer percentage; instead, the next items are other product links (Deluxe, self‑employed, etc.). The required disclosure text (“~37% of filers qualify. Simple Form 1040 returns only…”) does not appear anywhere on the page. Thus axis A is not satisfied. Because no eligibility disclosure is present, the wording cannot match the approved library entry, so axis B is also not satisfied. A possible counter‑argument is that this is just a navigational footer link and not a marketing claim, and therefore should not trigger the rule; however, the rule as written applies whenever TurboTax Free is mentioned on the page, and does not exempt navigation or footer mentions. Therefore this is treated as a triggered, non‑compliant instance.

findings.location: footer navigation under “Online Software Products” list
findings.reasoning: Trigger met because the page explicitly mentions the TurboTax Free product via the “Free Edition tax filing” link. The rule requires the disclosure “~37% of filers qualify. Simple Form 1040 returns only (no schedules, except for EITC, CTC, student loan interest, and Schedule 1-A).” right underneath such a mention. No such disclosure appears underneath this mention or elsewhere on the page, so Axis A is not compliant. Axis B does not match the approved library entry since the disclosure is absent. Strongest counter-reading: one could argue a navigational link does not constitute a marketing mention requiring the disclosure, or that “Start for free” CTAs are generic and not the Free product. However, the explicit product text “Free Edition” is present, and the rule does not exempt nav mentions; thus the requirement applies and is unmet.
